Hey, what's going on team? All right, so I wanted to make this video for you guys because a few of you had asked for beliefs to be broken. And so I wanted to take a moment to kind of reinforce those things for you and give you the conviction to feel good about this. So first off, above everything, he who can spend the most to acquire customer will win and the people who can provide the most value to end users will win. And the new business model, if you're looking at, let's say for example, you have 200 members at your gym and let's say last month you took home $8,000, right? If you have 200 members and you took home $8,000, then it means that how much you're taking home per customer was only, what is that, $10 times four. So you took home $40 per customer per month in terms of what you took home in your pocket. And that's at you taking home $8,000 a month if you have 200 members, right? Now, here's why this is important, okay? So whenever you're analyzing any kind of new opportunity, you have to look at what's the upside and what's the downside, right? The upside is obviously there's significantly more profit, right? From a revenue standpoint, the revenue opportunity is the same as you have with the gym, if not higher because people value one on one more. But even if we were just to say the revenue opportunity is the same, then you have the same absolute upside, number one. Two, you have four times the profit upside. Number three, you no longer are limited by your physical space, which means you could conceivably actually even downsize your space if you so desired. But your physical space no longer and your session times being packed, no longer limit how much you can earn, all right? That's huge, so that's upside, right? Now, downside, making this switch, what's the fear, right? So the fear is, if I do this, I might go out of business, right, that's the fear. But the reality is this model has lower risk. It has lower liability because the only thing you're accountable for here is just your rent. Everything else is something that you can choose to add in later if you so desire, right? And so, like, I'm trying to say this clearly, but like, you were exposed to less risk, it is riskier to not change to a lower risk model. And so it is pure fear, like, not even like, oh, there is potential to change, but like, the potential of change is the thought that what if this then makes me go out of business, but the thing that would make you go out of business is the overhead from the old model. So the new model does not have the overhead which would cause you to go out of business, which is why you shouldn't have the fear, right? And so, the reality is that like, the marketplace, and so here's the reality side. Workouts are commoditized, period, they are. Here's two reasons why. First, go to Equinox, go to LA Fitness, go to 24-hour Fitness, they have group workouts, they have caught on, they have their X-Fit training area where they hold classes that are just like yours, and they have equal access to trainers just like yours that you do. So as long as you're not the one teaching the classes, which hopefully anyone who's in here isn't, you don't have a better opportunity to get trainers, right? And so, the next thing is that the economy is going to crash. It might not be, it might not, six months, it might be 12 months, it might be 24 months, it might be three years, who knows? But it's going to go down. And when that happens, the commoditized industries are going to go under, right? And the new model, the one I'm talking about, the lower risk model, the lower overhead model, the higher profit margin model, are the ones who are going to be able to survive that, right? Furthermore, workouts, and this is from an impact standpoint, workouts are only 10% of the equation. We all know this. We know that people working out at our gym, if they don't change their food, which we know, because people who've been at your gym for a year, still look the same and they lost the weight and they're challenged and they stay the same after that. So we know that the workouts are not the thing that drive the change. The things that drive the change are the coaching and the food, right? And so, lo and behold, those are the things that we're selling now with more emphasis on it. And so, if you're worried about, I don't know if I'm going to help these people, you're going to help them more, right? The other thing, and this is a realization that I had, is that in order to make the gym model work that we have right now, and this is a huge epiphany for me, is that in order to make our existing model work, and mind you, this is all the stuff that only stays within this group, okay? In order to make our model work, we had to build a very operational heavy retention system. And what is the retention system that we have? It's relationship building, it's coaching. We attempt to have a one-on-one relationship with all the customers, right? And that's what makes them value the membership, which means we had to build a coaching model on top of our fitness model to get them to value the fitness when the reality is they just don't value it. And yet, all of our overhead is from the thing they don't value. So it's like, we built this thing that they do value, that they will spend the money for, and that's why they stay, is the coaching, the accountability, and the retention, and their relationships, right? And they don't care about the fitness, which is what all of our overhead is. So why don't we just get rid of the thing that is all the overhead, so that we can have the thing that they really want, and that they are clearly, and the people who are selling this already can see that they actually value. When people are getting at the end of a credit-only sale and saying 600, that's it, it means like we're selling the right thing. They value that. They're getting one-on-one training for $600. It's like, it's out of this world from an offer standpoint, and we will increase the price later, but for now, we should create consistency in process, right? Now, from a selling standpoint, because Christa was like, what do I do when someone walks in his referral? You should feel convicted that if they actually want to change their lives, coaching one-on-one is gonna do that better for them. I know firsthand, and maybe some of you guys know firsthand that one-on-one coaching has been a single thing that has allowed me to excel faster than anything else in my life. Coaching has, right? Not group workouts, coaching, breaking beliefs like what I'm doing right now, all right? And so if we want to truly help them, truly help them, not just have a workout facility that's fun, but truly help them, then we are tasked with breaking their beliefs so that we can actually change their lives. And so if you believe that, and when you started this business, you wanted to transform lives, you know that you can't transform someone's life in 4.3 months, which is what most people's average lifespan is, right? You're not gonna do that. The only way you can do that is through educating them and through holding them accountable and breaking their beliefs, which is really only possible in a one-on-one setting, the way that we are fulfilling it now, and it just so happens that it's four times as profitable for us. And so I feel like there's this level of guilt that people are having right now, where it's like, I'm only selling this because I'm making more money. That's limiting beliefs around money. The fact is they value it more because it is more valuable. The game has changed, right? The reason workouts are commoditized is because communication has been desynchronized, right? It means that any person can go on and make a YouTube channel and have millions of subscribers on their amazing yoga workouts. And they're free for everyone, which means that it's really difficult for you to compete on that planet, that space, right? But what you can compete on is being a one-on-one coach and doing that at system and at scale and doing that well, because that's what's going to change people's lives. Believe it or not, this is me just saying a hunch here, but the first hunch was right about this play. You're going to have lower churn with your one-on-one coaching clients than you do from your gym. So you're actually gonna have a higher ticket with five times the margin and lower churn. It's literally the best of everything with no risk or significantly less risk than you were exposed to before, right? And so anyways, I just wanted to give you kind of like the overarching picture of like why this is important. If you want to help people at a higher level, one-on-one coaching is the way. Truth, right? The fact that it happens to make you more money is just what makes it a commercial reality and what will allow us, everyone who's in this group, to outcompete and position yourself for the changes that are going to come. Like if you look at the industry, first it was commercial leasing, which is like the LA Fitness Big Box model, right? After that, they started adding in personal training and then the boom of personal training happened, right? After that, the economy crashed and then personal training 101 became outdoor boot camps because the people, the personal trainers who had the one-on-one relationships kept the one-on-one relationships and said, hey, you can pay less and come work out with me outside, right? And so they worked out with them outside. And then they were like, huh, I could provide more value if I had a studio. And that's when the boutique model started blowing up. And then after that, we realized, because now at this point, the people who got into the game were not personal trainers who transitioned, most people then started to open boutiques and started working. But if you follow the origin, right? It used to be personal trainers who had the one-on-one relationship and then the boutique started and then this whole workout craze started and then it became commoditized and priced, priced and monetized, which you guys all know, right? And that's when we introduced accountability and nutrition in order to get a higher price when you make the business model work. But the reality is that is the thing that they have ultimately always wanted. And now with technology, we can do that at scale and we can do it well. And we can do it in such a way that people actually value it. And for $77 a week, which is $331 a month, not including, notwithstanding the fact that your uptake rate on product, food supplements is going to be even higher than that. You're gonna be making $400 ahead, take home, right? And so right now, if you had 200 customers and you're taking home $8,000 a month net, you're making 40 bucks a customer. If you have the exact same business model, sorry, the exact same brick and mortar, right? Radius, except now you can take home $400 a month per person.
